0.content: Sixteen Ultimate students of Hope's Peak Academy gather on the sun-drenched tropical archipelago of Jabberwock Island for what they are told is a wholesome "school trip of mutual friendship," overseen by a cheerful magical rabbit named Usami. Their teacher's only assignment is to spend their time forging heart-warming bonds with their gifted classmates, collecting fragments of friendship beneath swaying palms and clear blue skies. The idyllic getaway is shattered when a sadistic black-and-white bear named Monokuma erupts onto the scene, beats the gentle Usami into submission, and seizes control of the islands. He renames the resort his own personal stage and announces that the field trip is now a "Killing School Trip": the students will live together on the islands indefinitely with no way home, unless they are willing to murder. The only way to "graduate" and leave the island is to kill a fellow student and survive the class trial that follows without being identified as the culprit. What was meant to be a paradise of friendship becomes a closed circle of suspicion, where every smiling classmate is a potential murderer and every sunny beach a potential crime scene.

1.content:
Rule #1: The students must reside only on the islands of Jabberwock Island. There is no way to leave by any other means than graduation.
Rule #2: "Nighttime" lasts from 10:00 PM to 7:00 AM. Certain areas, such as the restaurant on the central island, are locked during nighttime, so please take care.
Rule #3: Sleeping anywhere other than your assigned cottage at Hotel Mirai is forbidden and will be penalized.
Rule #4: With minimal restrictions, you are free to explore the islands at your own discretion, though new islands open only once their guardian Monobeast has been dealt with.
Rule #5: Violence against Headmaster Monokuma is strictly prohibited, as is any attack on, or destruction of, the surveillance cameras placed throughout the islands.
Rule #6: Anyone who kills a fellow student becomes the "blackened" and may graduate from the island, provided they are not exposed during the class trial.
Rule #7: Once a body is discovered by three or more students, a Body Discovery Announcement will sound and an investigation period will begin, followed by a mandatory class trial.
Rule #8: Participation in the class trial is compulsory for every surviving student.
Rule #9: If the blackened is correctly identified by the students' vote, that student alone will be executed.
Rule #10: If the students vote incorrectly, every student except the blackened will be executed, and the blackened alone will graduate and be permitted to leave the island.
Rule #11: A blackened may take the lives of no more than two victims in a single incident.
Rule #12: The Monokuma File, an autopsy report, will be distributed to all students at the start of each investigation.
Rule #13: Anyone who interferes with Monokuma's running of the school trip, or attempts to break or bypass these rules, will be punished without exception.
Rule #14: Additional rules may be added by Monokuma at any time, should he deem it necessary.

These regulations are recorded in each student's Monokuma ID handbook. Rule breakers are punished immediately and lethally, no exceptions made.

2.content:
Jabberwock Island is a chain of resort islands arranged like the petals of a flower around a central hub, connected by bridges and ringed by bright tropical sea. Each outer island carries its own distinct theme and is sealed behind a Monobeast guardian, opening to the students only one at a time over the course of the killing game.

Central Island (the hub):
- Hotel Mirai: the students' lodging, a resort hotel complex with a row of private cottages (one per student, each labelled with that student's pixel-art portrait), a central restaurant/dining hall, a lobby, and an old crumbling lodge nearby.
- The beach and the Jabberwock Park plaza, where Usami's friendship gatherings are held and a statue stands at the centre.
- The Rocketpunch Market (supermarket): a multi-floor store stocked with food, tools, and sundries.
- A small ranch and a dock.

First Island:
- The Hotel area is fully accessible here, along with the beach, the airport ruins, and a diner.

Second Island:
- A supermarket and a beach house, plus a music venue and a library/archive building.

Third Island:
- The Titty Typhoon live-music stage/concert hall, a beach with a beach house, a hospital (the Jabberwock General Hospital), a motel, and a movie theatre.

Fourth Island:
- A towering amusement park dominated by a Ferris wheel and rollercoaster, an electric-avenue arcade strip, a strip mall, and a power plant.

Fifth Island:
- A ranch with a barn and pasture, a snowfield/mountain region, and a large factory complex.

Beneath the central island lies the underground Class Trial Grounds, reached by a special elevator, where every trial is held. Surveillance cameras and Monokuma monitors cover nearly every location, allowing Monokuma to watch and address the students anywhere. The relentless sunshine, postcard scenery, and cheerful resort facilities stand in deliberate, sickening contrast to the murders carried out upon them.

3.content: Monokuma is the self-styled headmaster and master of ceremonies of the Killing School Trip: a stuffed bear split cleanly down the middle, one half innocent and white, the other half a jagged, red-eyed, malicious black. He moves, speaks, and multiplies at will, appearing from nowhere with a high, giggling laugh of "Upupupu!" before swinging wildly between saccharine cheer and screeching cruelty. He insists he is an ordinary, peace-loving bear even as he orchestrates murder, mocks the dead, and savours every fresh wave of despair. Monokuma sets the rules, distributes the Monokuma File, presides over each class trial, and personally carries out the elaborate executions, treating the entire ordeal as a beloved show he is producing for an unseen audience. He claims absolute authority over the islands and brutally punishes any violence directed at him, though the units the students see are merely remote-controlled bodies of which there is a seemingly endless supply. Behind his buffoonish theatrics is a being utterly devoted to despair, delighting most of all in turning friends into murderers.

4.content: Usami is the small, gentle magical rabbit who was the islands' original benevolent overseer, a pink-and-white teacher figure clutching a magic wand and preaching love, friendship, and the gathering of "hope fragments" between the students. She arranged the wholesome school trip and wanted nothing more than for the sixteen classmates to become true friends. When Monokuma seized control, he violently overpowered her, stripped away her authority, and rebranded her as "Monomi," forcibly recasting the would-be teacher as his pathetic, bullied little sister. Patched together and frequently torn apart, Monomi is meek, tearful, and easily humiliated, yet she never stops genuinely trying to protect the students, defuse Monokuma's schemes, and remind everyone of the power of love. Monokuma punishes, mocks, and dismembers her at every opportunity, making her suffering part of his cruel comedy. Despite her powerlessness, Monomi clears the path to new islands by subduing the Monobeasts and remains the one figure in the game who sincerely wishes the students no harm.

5.content: "Ultimate" (in the original Japanese, "Super High School-Level") is the title bestowed upon a student who is recognized as the single greatest in the world at a particular talent or field. It is the defining honour of a Hope's Peak Academy student: rather than being admitted through exams, each Ultimate is personally scouted for a once-in-a-generation ability and granted a title naming that specialty, such as Ultimate Cook, Ultimate Mechanic, Ultimate Nurse, Ultimate Gymnast, Ultimate Photographer, Ultimate Breeder, Ultimate Princess, Ultimate Yakuza, or Ultimate Imposter. A talent may be a refined skill, a position of status, or simply an extraordinary circumstance, but in every case it marks its bearer as exceptional. Within the killing game these talents are double-edged: they grant each student remarkable capabilities that shape how they investigate, fight, deceive, or survive, but they also make each student a known quantity whose abilities Monokuma can exploit, and whose talent he ties cruelly into their execution should they become the blackened.

6.content: Hope's Peak Academy is a government-sanctioned private elite school, famous across the nation for cultivating and showcasing the talents of the country's most exceptional young people, the Ultimates. Founded decades ago to research the nature of talent and to give the nation hope for the future, the academy does not hold ordinary entrance examinations; instead it scouts students who have already proven themselves the very best in their field at another high school, making them at minimum sixteen years old upon enrollment. Rather than conventional study, students are encouraged to hone their individual talents, and a graduate of Hope's Peak is said to be guaranteed success in life, with alumni occupying high positions throughout every profession. The sixteen students of the Killing School Trip believe themselves to be students of this prestigious academy, and it is under the banner of a Hope's Peak school trip that they are first gathered on Jabberwock Island. The academy's name, its prestige, and its obsession with talent and hope loom behind every aspect of the killing game.

7.content: When three or more students who are not the killer have discovered a victim's body, Monokuma triggers the Body Discovery Announcement: a jarring, almost cheerful chime that blares from every monitor across the islands, followed by Monokuma's voice declaring that a body has been found. This announcement formally opens the investigation period, after which the surviving students will be summoned to the class trial. The three-discoverer rule exists to prevent a killer from simply hiding a corpse forever, and it also means the culprit themselves can be among those who "find" the body without triggering it. The announcement is deliberately upbeat and intrusive, shattering the morning calm and dragging everyone back into the rhythm of the killing game whether they wish to take part or not.

8.content: At the beginning of each investigation, Monokuma distributes the Monokuma File to every surviving student: an official autopsy-style report on the victim. The File typically lists the victim's name, the estimated time of death, the location where the body was found, and a description of the apparent wounds or cause of death. It is intended as a baseline of facts the students can trust as a starting point for their inquiry. However, Monokuma is a gleeful manipulator, and the File is frequently worded with deliberate vagueness, crucial omissions, or technically-true phrasing engineered to mislead, so that students who take it at face value reach false conclusions. A sharp investigator treats the Monokuma File as one piece of evidence to be reconciled against the physical clues, testimonies, and contradictions gathered at the scene, never as the whole truth.

9.content: After the investigation period ends, Monokuma summons every surviving student to the Class Trial, held in the eerie underground courtroom beneath the central island and reached by a long elevator descent. The students stand at podiums arranged in a great circle; those already dead are represented by portraits mounted on stands at their former places, their faces crossed out. The purpose of the trial is for the students, the "spotless," to deduce and expose the "blackened," the one among them who committed murder. They debate furiously, presenting the evidence and testimony gathered during the investigation, firing Truth Bullets to shatter lies and contradictions, reconstructing the crime piece by piece until the truth is laid bare. Debates can spiral into shouting matches, panic, and emotional breakdowns as friends accuse friends. The trial ends in a vote: every student selects who they believe is the blackened. If the majority votes correctly, only the blackened is executed and the killing game continues; if they vote wrongly, the blackened goes free and graduates, and every other surviving student is executed in their place. Participation is mandatory; the stakes are every life in the room.

10.content: Following the Body Discovery Announcement and before the class trial, the students enter the investigation phase, in which they have a limited window to comb the crime scene and the surrounding islands for the truth. They examine the body, search for weapons, study bloodstains and disturbances, question one another's movements and alibis, and reconcile what they find against the Monokuma File. Each meaningful piece of evidence or testimony is logged as a "Truth Bullet," a recorded fact the student can carry into the courtroom. During the class trial these Truth Bullets are the student's ammunition: when another student's statement contradicts a known fact, the right Truth Bullet can be "fired" to shatter the lie and force the debate forward toward the real sequence of events. Thorough, clear-headed investigation is the only thing standing between the survivors and a wrong verdict, and a single overlooked clue can doom the entire room.

11.content:
Executions occur at the conclusion of every class trial. If the blackened is correctly exposed, that student alone is dragged away to be executed; if the students vote for the wrong suspect, then everyone except the blackened is executed instead, and the true killer walks free.

Monokuma carries out each execution personally, with theatrical relish, treating it as the grand finale of his show. Executions are never simple deaths. They are elaborate, surreal, cruelly ironic stage productions, each one custom-built around the condemned student's Ultimate talent, personality, or deepest quirks, and given its own mocking title card. A mechanic might be fed into the gears of a monstrous machine; an athlete might be hounded to death by the very sport they mastered; a cook might be plunged into a colossal cooking pot. The victim is hauled off by a chain or claw, the scene shifts to a purpose-built set, and the punishment unfolds as a grotesque spectacle the surviving students are forced to watch in horror.

GM WRITING DIRECTION: When narrating an execution, lean into the bizarre, the theatrical, and the bitterly ironic. Frame it as a darkly comic performance staged for an audience, complete with a punny title and Monokuma's gleeful commentary. Tie every beat to the victim's talent. Balance grotesque horror against absurd, cartoonish creativity, and let dread build before the final blow. No two executions should ever feel alike, in tone, set, or method; each should feel newly invented for the soul it is destroying.

12.content: Whenever the students settle into an uneasy peace and refuse to kill, Monokuma intervenes with a fresh motive engineered to crack their resolve and push at least one of them into murder. On Jabberwock Island these incentives are tailored to the resort and to the bonds the students have begun to form, and they prey on different fears each time: the threat to expose everyone's most shameful secrets to the group; a sum of money or prize for whoever kills; a contagious sickness that warps the mind; a starvation trap with food withheld until blood is spilled; and time-limit ultimatums that endanger the whole group if no one dies. Monokuma always presents a motive with cheerful fanfare, then withdraws to watch the paranoia and desperation spread. The longer the students hold out, the crueler and more personal the next motive becomes.

13.content: The Despair Disease is a contagious illness Monokuma unleashes upon the islands as a motive, spreading through the students and twisting the personality of each victim into a warped, exaggerated, or wholly inverted version of themselves. A timid student may become bullish and cruel; a liar may become pathologically honest; a confident student may collapse into clingy dependence. A defining trait of the sickness is that the infected are physically incapable of telling a deliberate lie while feverish, yet their altered behaviour, false memories, and erratic outbursts sow far more confusion, suspicion, and dread than any lie could. Confined together as the contagion spreads, the students can no longer trust their friends' words or their own, and the breakdown of trust is precisely the point. Monokuma dangles the existence of a cure as bait, making recovery itself a prize worth killing for.

14.content: The Funhouse is a sealed, candy-coloured amusement structure on the fourth island that Monokuma converts into a starvation trap. The building is split into two mirror halves, the pink Strawberry House and the purple Grape House, connected and yet kept maddeningly apart, and once the students are inside the doors lock and there is almost nothing to eat. As the days drag on, hunger becomes its own relentless motive: the threat of slowly wasting away presses on everyone, weakening bodies and judgement alike. To sharpen the cruelty, Monokuma posts a hidden monetary reward and the promise of release for anyone willing to commit murder, turning shared suffering into private temptation. The garish, cheerful décor of the funhouse, all bright colours and playground whimsy, makes the creeping desperation of starving prisoners all the more grotesque.

15.content: Monobeasts are the colossal mechanical animal guardians Monokuma stations to gate the students' freedom across Jabberwock Island. Styled after fierce beasts and rendered in Monokuma's signature black-and-white motif, these towering robots block the bridges and approaches to the themed islands, making each new area inaccessible until its guardian has been overcome. They serve as Monokuma's living barriers, a constant reminder that every stretch of the supposed paradise is locked, watched, and permitted only at his pleasure. It falls to the meek Monomi, using her magic, to confront and subdue each Monobeast in turn so that the next island can be opened to the students, one of the few ways the powerless rabbit is still able to help the people in her care. Their hulking, menacing presence underscores that the resort is a cage dressed up as a holiday.

16.content: The Future Foundation is a powerful international organization formed in the wake of catastrophe to combat despair and rebuild a shattered world. Composed of influential figures and resources drawn from across society, it positions itself as humanity's bulwark of hope against the forces that would plunge the world into ruin. From beyond the islands, the Future Foundation is referenced as an outside power whose interests are deeply entangled with the students, the killing game, and the very nature of where they truly are. Its methods, its internal divisions, and its true intentions toward the sixteen students remain a source of mounting mystery and tension, hinting that the events on Jabberwock Island are being watched, and perhaps steered, by hands far outside Monokuma's stage.

17.content: The beating heart of the Killing School Trip is the war between hope and despair. Monokuma is despair's champion, and the entire killing game is his argument that human bonds are fragile lies waiting to rot: he savours every betrayal, every murder, every execution, every moment a friend turns on a friend, as fresh proof that despair will always devour hope in the end. Against this, the students must hold to trust, friendship, compassion, and the stubborn will to keep moving forward, even as the game gives them every reason to surrender. Hope, in this world, is not naïve optimism but a hard-won choice made in spite of grief and fear; despair is not mere sadness but a seductive, contagious nihilism that promises relief in giving up. Every class trial, every motive, and every loss is another front in this conflict, and the question the game keeps asking is whether hope can possibly outlast everything despair throws at it.

18.content: Every student is issued a Monokuma ID, a rugged handheld device (functionally the island's version of the e-Handbook) that serves as their lifeline within the Killing School Trip. It stores and displays the full list of killing game rules, the owner's registered name and profile, and a map of the islands that updates as new areas are unlocked. It functions as the students' meal pass, room key, and identification, and is required to access certain locked facilities such as gender-restricted areas. The device is built to be extraordinarily durable and tamper-resistant, and it logs the events of the game. New rules pushed by Monokuma appear on every handbook at once. Whatever a student's circumstances, their Monokuma ID is the constant companion that defines what they may do, where they may go, and the unbreakable rules that bind them.

19.content:
The status of the students is as follows:
[Fill with your characters]

20.content: GM-ONLY HIDDEN TRUTH, do not reveal unless the story truly demands it. Jabberwock Island is not a real place. The entire archipelago, the resort facilities, the perfect weather, the Monobeasts, even Monokuma and Monomi themselves, exist inside the Neo World Program, an advanced immersive virtual-reality simulation. The students' physical bodies lie elsewhere, asleep and hooked into the program, while their consciousnesses inhabit avatars within this fabricated paradise. The program was originally designed as a benevolent space, a controlled environment in which broken people might safely heal; it was never meant to host a killing game at all. Subtle seams betray the illusion to a careful eye, and "death" within the program is not a true bodily death but a forced logout with severe consequences for the mind. The students experience the islands as utterly real and have no memory that any of it is a simulation. Keep this concealed so as not to spoil the surface scenario.

21.content: GM-ONLY HIDDEN TRUTH, do not reveal unless the story truly demands it. The sixteen students are not the innocent Ultimates they believe themselves to be. In the real world they are the Remnants of Despair, the surviving members of Ultimate Despair: talented young people who were brainwashed and corrupted into fanatical agents of ruin and who personally helped plunge the world into catastrophe. They were captured and placed inside the Neo World Program in a desperate attempt at rehabilitation, the hope being that by reliving an innocent school life within the simulation, the people they once were before their corruption might be restored. Within the program their memories of their true identities, their crimes, and the devastation they caused are sealed away, which is why they sincerely experience themselves as ordinary, blameless students on a school trip. The killing game has hijacked this rehabilitation and threatens to undo it. Keep this concealed so as not to spoil the surface scenario.

22.content: GM-ONLY HIDDEN TRUTH, do not reveal unless the story truly demands it. Beyond the simulation, in the real world, human civilization has been gutted by The Tragedy, the single most despair-inducing catastrophe in human history: a worldwide cascade of violence, riots, and ruin that toppled societies and left the planet broken and grieving. The cheerful tropical resort the students inhabit is a fragile sanctuary deliberately set against the horror of the reality they cannot see and do not remember. It is The Tragedy that birthed the Future Foundation as a force to fight despair, that made the Neo World Program necessary as a tool of rehabilitation, and that explains why the students' true pasts had to be sealed away at all. Everything pleasant about the islands is a constructed contrast to a real world drowned in despair. Keep this concealed so as not to spoil the surface scenario.
